The **Spokane Indians** are a [Minor League Baseball](/wiki/Minor_League_Baseball "Minor League Baseball") team located in [Spokane Valley](/wiki/Spokane_Valley,_Washington "Spokane Valley, Washington"), the city immediately east of [Spokane](/wiki/Spokane "Spokane"), [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)"), in the [Pacific Northwest](/wiki/Pacific_Northwest "Pacific Northwest"). The Indians are members of the [High-A](/wiki/High-A "High-A") [Northwest League](/wiki/Northwest_League "Northwest League") (NWL) as an affiliate of the [Colorado Rockies](/wiki/Colorado_Rockies "Colorado Rockies"). Spokane plays its home games at [Avista Stadium](/wiki/Avista_Stadium "Avista Stadium"), which opened in 1958 and has a seating capacity of 6,752.

From 1958 through 1982, excluding 1972, the Indians were in the [Triple-A](/wiki/Triple-A_(baseball) "Triple-A (baseball)") [Pacific Coast League](/wiki/Pacific_Coast_League "Pacific Coast League") (PCL). They were members of the [Class A Short Season](/wiki/Class_A_Short_Season "Class A Short Season") Northwest League from 1955 to 1956, in 1972, and from 1983 to 2020. The NWL operated as the [High-A West](/wiki/High-A_West "High-A West") in 2021 and was elevated to the High-A level. They have won 12 league titles: four in the PCL and nine in the NWL. The Spokane region has over a century of history in Minor League Baseball, dating back to the 1890s.[[2]](#cite_note-bothin-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spokane_Indians&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Before 1958

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spokane_Indians&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Before 1958")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/1909_Spokane_Indians.jpeg/250px-1909_Spokane_Indians.jpeg)](/wiki/File:1909_Spokane_Indians.jpeg)

The 1909 Spokane Indians

Spokane's minor league history dates to 1890, when it fielded a team in the [Pacific Northwest League](/wiki/Pacific_Northwest_League "Pacific Northwest League"). The Spokane Club won the Northwest League pennant in its first season, overcoming teams from Portland, Seattle, and Tacoma, among others. The nickname Indians dates to 1903, when Spokane joined the [Pacific National League](/wiki/Pacific_National_League "Pacific National League"), a predecessor to the [Pacific Coast League](/wiki/Pacific_Coast_League "Pacific Coast League") and, at [Class A](/wiki/Class_A_(baseball) "Class A (baseball)"), an elite minor league of the period, equivalent to [Triple-A](/wiki/Triple-A_(baseball) "Triple-A (baseball)") today. The Indians lasted only two seasons at that higher level before dropping to the [Class B](/wiki/Class_B_(baseball) "Class B (baseball)") [Northwestern League](/wiki/Northwestern_League "Northwestern League"), which folded during World War I.

In 1937, Spokane became a charter member of the Class B [Western International League](/wiki/Western_International_League "Western International League") (WIL),[[3]](#cite_note-begsadend-3) the predecessor of the Northwest League. They played at [Ferris Field](/wiki/Playfair_Race_Course#Ferris_Field "Playfair Race Course") from 1937 through 1942 and 1946 until folding during the 1954 season on June 21.[[4]](#cite_note-wtosaw-4)[[5]](#cite_note-cronwet-5)[[6]](#cite_note-irhntptl-6)

Spokane was a charter member of the Northwest League, which debuted in 1955 as a Class B league. These Indians also played at Ferris Field,[[7]](#cite_note-cntiopnr55-7)[[8]](#cite_note-tdpfnle-8) but folded after just two seasons, and the city went without minor league baseball in 1957.[[9]](#cite_note-spffeb57-9)

#### The 1946 Spokane bus tragedy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spokane_Indians&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: The 1946 Spokane bus tragedy")]

In 1946, the WIL Indians were victims of the worst transit accident in the history of American professional sport. On June 24, the team was on its way west to [Bremerton](/wiki/Bremerton,_Washington "Bremerton, Washington") by bus to play the Bluejackets. While crossing the [Cascade Mountains](/wiki/Cascade_Mountains "Cascade Mountains") on a rain-slickened [Snoqualmie Pass](/wiki/Snoqualmie_Pass "Snoqualmie Pass") Highway (then [U.S. Route 10](/wiki/U.S._Route_10_in_Washington "U.S. Route 10 in Washington")), the bus driver swerved to avoid an oncoming car. The Indians' vehicle veered off the road and down an embankment, then crashed and burst into flames.[[3]](#cite_note-begsadend-3)[[10]](#cite_note-esbpdic-10)

Nine men died—six of them instantly—and seven were injured. Many of the injured had burn injuries. The dead were catcher/manager Mel Cole (age 32), pitchers Bob Kinnaman (28) and George Lyden (23), catcher [Chris Hartje](/wiki/Chris_Hartje "Chris Hartje") (31),[[11]](#cite_note-hdsid-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) infielders Fred Martinez (24), Vic Picetti (18) and George Risk (25), and outfielders Bob James (25) and Bob Paterson (23). Despite a severe head wound, infielder [Ben Geraghty](/wiki/Ben_Geraghty "Ben Geraghty") was able to struggle back up the mountainside to signal for help. Injured survivors also included pitchers Pete Barisoff, Gus Hallbourg and Dick Powers, catcher Irv Konopka, outfielder Levi McCormack, and bus driver Glen Berg.[[13]](#cite_note-koty-13)

One player from the 1946 team, future major league infielder [Jack "Lucky" Lohrke](/wiki/Jack_%22Lucky%22_Lohrke "Jack \"Lucky\" Lohrke"), missed the tragedy because his contract was sold to the PCL [San Diego Padres](/wiki/San_Diego_Padres_(PCL) "San Diego Padres (PCL)") on June 24 and he departed the ill-fated bus during a late lunch stop in [Ellensburg](/wiki/Ellensburg,_Washington "Ellensburg, Washington"), not long before the accident, thus helping to earn his nickname.[[14]](#cite_note-sitbcosp-14) (Lohrke had previously averted tragedy when he was bumped from a military transport plane which later crashed.) Two Indians' pitchers, Milt Cadinha and Joe Faria, were making the trip to Bremerton by automobile and were not aboard the team bus when it crashed.[[13]](#cite_note-koty-13)

The Indians, relying on players loaned from other teams, managed to finish the season and placed seventh in the league. A special charity, the Spokane Baseball Benefit Association, donated $114,800 to the injured survivors and dependents of the nine players who died.[[13]](#cite_note-koty-13)

Beth Bollinger of Spokane wrote a novel titled *Until the End of the Ninth*, which is based on the true story of the 1946 bus crash and its aftermath.

### Pacific Coast League (1958–1971, 1973–1982)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spokane_Indians&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Pacific Coast League (1958–1971, 1973–1982)")]

When the [Los Angeles Dodgers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Dodgers "Los Angeles Dodgers") moved from [Brooklyn](/wiki/1957_Brooklyn_Dodgers_season "1957 Brooklyn Dodgers season") to the [west coast](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States") in [1958](/wiki/1958_Los_Angeles_Dodgers_season "1958 Los Angeles Dodgers season"), they moved their PCL affiliate, the [Los Angeles Angels](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Angels_(PCL) "Los Angeles Angels (PCL)"), north to Spokane. While with the Dodgers for 14 seasons, the Indians won league titles in 1960 and 1970, and were runners-up in 1963, 1967, and 1968.

The 1970 Indians, managed by [Tommy Lasorda](/wiki/Tommy_Lasorda "Tommy Lasorda"),[[15]](#cite_note-pobdi-15) won 94 of 146 games (.644) in the regular season to win the northern division by 26 games,[[16]](#cite_note-nsvtyfs-16) then swept the [Hawaii Islanders](/wiki/Hawaii_Islanders "Hawaii Islanders") in four games in the PCL playoffs.[[17]](#cite_note-swpoisl-17) The team included [Bill Buckner](/wiki/Bill_Buckner "Bill Buckner"), [Steve Garvey](/wiki/Steve_Garvey "Steve Garvey"), [Bobby Valentine](/wiki/Bobby_Valentine "Bobby Valentine") (PCL MVP), [Tom Paciorek](/wiki/Tom_Paciorek "Tom Paciorek"), [Davey Lopes](/wiki/Davey_Lopes "Davey Lopes"), [Bill Russell](/wiki/Bill_Russell_(shortstop) "Bill Russell (shortstop)"), and [Doyle Alexander](/wiki/Doyle_Alexander "Doyle Alexander").[[17]](#cite_note-swpoisl-17)[[18]](#cite_note-bvgcmvp-18)[[19]](#cite_note-spoinsvnt-19)

Following the 1971 season, the club was moved south to [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico") and became the [Albuquerque Dukes](/wiki/Albuquerque_Dukes "Albuquerque Dukes"). Spokane, which had been in the Northwest League for its first two seasons in 1955 and 1956, returned to the NWL in 1972 as a Dodger affiliate,[[20]](#cite_note-bbsfspok-20)[[21]](#cite_note-kmhdsclb-21) but only for one season, as a new PCL franchise arrived in 1973 from [Portland](/wiki/Portland_Beavers "Portland Beavers"), becoming the affiliate of the [Texas Rangers](/wiki/Texas_Rangers_(baseball) "Texas Rangers (baseball)").[[22]](#cite_note-expow-22) The 1973 team, which included [Bill Madlock](/wiki/Bill_Madlock "Bill Madlock") and [Lenny Randle](/wiki/Lenny_Randle "Lenny Randle"), won the west division by eleven games and swept [Tucson](/wiki/Tucson_Toros "Tucson Toros") in three games in the championship series.[[23]](#cite_note-sedate-23) The following year's club successfully defended the title with another three-game sweep, this time over Albuquerque.[[24]](#cite_note-spcapflg-24)

The Indians' second stint in Triple-A lasted ten seasons and included affiliations with the Rangers, which changed to the [Milwaukee Brewers](/wiki/Milwaukee_Brewers "Milwaukee Brewers") in 1976, [Seattle Mariners](/wiki/Seattle_Mariners "Seattle Mariners") in 1979, and [California Angels](/wiki/California_Angels "California Angels") in 1982. Taking their first division crown since 1974,[[25]](#cite_note-pclfstgs-25) the Indians defeated [Tacoma](/wiki/Tacoma_Rainiers "Tacoma Rainiers") in the first round,[[26]](#cite_note-trtpuduk-26) but fell to Albuquerque in the championship series in six games.[[27]](#cite_note-dwipagn-27) Soon after that season,[[28]](#cite_note-igonveg-28)[[29]](#cite_note-stmokdupi-29) the team moved south to [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas") and became the [Stars](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Stars_(baseball) "Las Vegas Stars (baseball)"). The team's general manager was [Larry Koentopp](/wiki/Larry_Koentopp "Larry Koentopp"), former head coach and athletic director at [Gonzaga](/wiki/Gonzaga_Bulldogs_baseball "Gonzaga Bulldogs baseball").[[30]](#cite_note-kqgbas-30) He was the leader of a local ownership group that purchased the team after the 1978 season.[[31]](#cite_note-trbpurlg-31)[[32]](#cite_note-fsebsfst-32) The team was purchased for $259,000 in 1978 and was sold in 1993 for $6.1 million.[[33]](#cite_note-ktfhdet-33)

### Northwest League (1983–2020)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spokane_Indians&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Northwest League (1983–2020)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Spokane_Indians_tag_out.jpg/250px-Spokane_Indians_tag_out.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spokane_Indians_tag_out.jpg)

Season opener at a 2008 Indians game against the [Everett AquaSox](/wiki/Everett_AquaSox "Everett AquaSox") at [Avista Stadium](/wiki/Avista_Stadium "Avista Stadium")

A new NWL franchise was awarded to Spokane for the 1983 season and the Indians have won eight league titles; the first four were consecutive, from 1987 through 1990.[[34]](#cite_note-iwrfst-34) The Indians won their seventh NWL championship in 2005, despite a 37–39 (.487) record during the regular season. They became only the second team in league history (after the 1982 [Salem Angels](/wiki/Salem_Angels "Salem Angels")) to win the championship with a losing regular season record. Spokane won the east division,[[35]](#cite_note-shswimpl-35)[[36]](#cite_note-nwlfs-36) then beat league-leading [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver_Canadians "Vancouver Canadians") on the road in games four and five of the championship series to win the title.[[37]](#cite_note-ioapp-37)[[38]](#cite_note-iwnwlpnt-38)

In 2008, the Indians captured their eighth league title with a thrilling four-game series victory over the [Salem-Keizer Volcanoes](/wiki/Salem-Keizer_Volcanoes "Salem-Keizer Volcanoes"). After dropping the first game, Spokane rallied to an 11–10 win in 10 innings to even the series. In game three, the Indians fell behind 10–2 before rallying for nine unanswered runs to win again 11–10. Spokane won the title with a 6–5 victory in 10 innings in the fourth game.

The Indians were featured in the "Spokane Alphabet" reverse glass painting by Washington artist Melinda Curtin. They were the "I" in the alphabet, cementing their place as an important part of the city of Spokane.

Following the 1985 season, the team was bought by the Brett brothers (John, [Ken](/wiki/Ken_Brett "Ken Brett"), Bobby, and [George](/wiki/George_Brett "George Brett")).[[39]](#cite_note-itsoffl-39)

### High-A West and back to the Northwest League (2021–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spokane_Indians&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: High-A West and back to the Northwest League (2021–present)")]

In conjunction with [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball")'s restructuring of Minor League Baseball in 2021, the Indians were organized into the [High-A West](/wiki/High-A_West "High-A West") along with five other teams previously of the Northwest League.[[40]](#cite_note-JMayo2-12-2021-40) They qualified for the playoffs by finishing with a second-place 67–49 record,[[41]](#cite_note-BR2021league-41) but they were defeated by the [Eugene Emeralds](/wiki/Eugene_Emeralds "Eugene Emeralds"), 3–1, in the best-of-five championship series.[[42]](#cite_note-42) The franchise was recognized with the [Minor League Baseball Organization of the Year Award](/wiki/Minor_League_Baseball_Organization_of_the_Year_Award "Minor League Baseball Organization of the Year Award").[[43]](#cite_note-43)

The High-A West was rebranded back to the Northwest League in March 2022, as MLB moved to revert all of its Minor Leagues to their historical names.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

## Logos and uniforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spokane_Indians&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Logos and uniforms")]

The team's colors are red, navy blue, light blue, and beige. In the 2006 offseason, the Indians began a process to redesign their logo and uniforms, looking to avoid the use of any American Indian imagery that might be perceived as derogatory. However, early in the process, the [Spokane Nation](/wiki/Spokane_people "Spokane people") contacted the team about offering their official support. In the process, the tribe gave permission to the team to adopt subtle and tasteful imagery, in order to pay homage to the team's history and new connection with the tribe. The cooperation included the creation of a secondary logo written in [Salish](/wiki/Salishan_languages "Salishan languages"), the traditional language of the Spokane.[[45]](#cite_note-Salish-45)
